notes: Chef review wave 10. Backstage source notes only; public pages should remain recipe-first.

recipes.review_summary: The old Sierra Leone Akara page used the broader black-eyed-pea fritter. This pass corrects it to Sierra Leonean banana akara made with ripe banana, rice flour, sugar, and frying oil.
recommended_changes: Use ripe bananas and rice flour instead of black-eyed peas.
Keep the batter thick enough to drop from a spoon.
Serve hot as breakfast or snack.
recipes.official_source_status: Sierra Leone specific sources consistently describe banana akara as rice-flour and mashed-banana fritters, distinct from bean akara elsewhere in West Africa.

sources.title: Roads & Kingdoms - Rice Akara in Freetown

recipe.name: Banana Akara
recipe.description: Sierra Leonean banana akara, sweet rice-flour fritters made with ripe banana, sugar, nutmeg, and hot oil.
recipe.story: This is Salone akara, not the bean fritter from elsewhere. The banana should be ripe enough to perfume the batter before it hits the oil.
recipe.best_served_with: Ginger beer, tea, onion gravy, bread, or a dusting of sugar

replace_steps.title: Mash bananas
replace_steps.instruction: Mash ripe bananas with sugar and nutmeg until mostly smooth.

replace_steps.title: Make batter
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in rice flour and baking powder if using. Add warm water only if needed to make a thick spoonable batter.

replace_steps.title: Rest batter
replace_steps.instruction: Let the batter rest so the rice flour hydrates and the mixture firms slightly.

replace_steps.title: Fry akara
replace_steps.instruction: Drop spoonfuls into hot oil and fry until golden on both sides.

replace_steps.title: Drain and serve
replace_steps.instruction: Drain well and serve hot with tea, ginger beer, bread, or onion gravy.

recipes.review_summary: Cassava leaf stew was accurate but needed a longer cook time and garlic or ogiri context. This pass keeps meat, smoked fish, palm oil, and groundnut while making the simmer more patient.
recommended_changes: Use finely pounded cassava leaves.
Cook meat separately and use the stock.
Simmer with palm oil, smoked fish, groundnut, onion, garlic, and chile until thick.
recipes.official_source_status: Sierra Leone references consistently describe cassava leaf stew as pounded cassava leaves cooked with palm oil, meat, smoked fish, groundnut or peanut butter, onions, and chile.

sources.title: Flavor365 - Sierra Leone Cassava Leaf Stew

recipe.description: Sierra Leonean cassava leaf stew with palm oil, beef or chicken, smoked fish, groundnut, onion, garlic, and chile.
recipe.story: Cassava leaves need time. The stew is ready when the greens are tender, the fish has seasoned the pot, and red oil rises gently at the edges.
recipe.best_served_with: White rice, plantain, fufu, or gari

replace_steps.title: Cook meat
replace_steps.instruction: Season meat with onion, salt, and pepper. Simmer until tender and reserve the stock.

replace_steps.title: Start leaves
replace_steps.instruction: Simmer cassava leaves in a little water until they soften and lose their raw edge.

replace_steps.title: Build stew
replace_steps.instruction: Heat palm oil and cook onion, garlic, chile, and ogiri if using. Add leaves, meat, smoked fish, and stock.

replace_steps.title: Add groundnut
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in ground peanuts or peanut butter and simmer until the stew thickens and oil rises.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Adjust salt and serve over white rice.

recipes.review_summary: Groundnut stew needed missing garlic, ginger, tomato paste, stock, and vegetable options. This pass keeps chicken and peanut as the center.
recommended_changes: Brown or boil chicken and keep the stock.
Cook onion, garlic, ginger, tomato, tomato paste, and chile before adding peanut.
Simmer until the oil separates and the sauce thickens.
recipes.official_source_status: Sierra Leone and West African references consistently describe groundnut stew as peanut paste or peanut butter cooked with chicken, tomato, onion, garlic, ginger, chile, and stock.

sources.title: Festival Medical Services - Sierra Leone Groundnut Soup

recipe.description: Sierra Leonean groundnut stew with chicken, peanut butter, tomato, onion, garlic, ginger, chile, and rich stock.
recipe.story: The peanut needs heat and patience. Simmer until the sauce turns glossy and thick enough to coat rice.
recipe.best_served_with: White rice, plantain, fufu, cassava, or gari

replace_ingredients.name: smooth peanut butter or groundnut paste
sweet potato, carrot, cabbage, or okra

recipes.review_summary: Bariis Iskukaris was solid, but the spice list should reflect xawaash more fully. This pass adds coriander, cloves, turmeric, and optional saffron or food color, and removes halal as a sourcing guarantee.
recommended_changes: Cook meat first and use its broth for the rice.
Use xawaash with cumin, coriander, cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, pepper, turmeric, and ginger.
Serve with banana and salad.
recipes.official_source_status: Somali references consistently describe bariis iskukaris as one-pot spiced rice with meat, xawaash, raisins, aromatics, and banana on the side.

recipe.description: Somali bariis iskukaris, spiced basmati rice cooked with goat or lamb broth, xawaash, caramelized onion, raisins, and warm spices.
recipe.story: The rice should taste like the meat broth and the spice pot together. Cook the meat first, then let the rice finish in that broth.
recipe.best_served_with: Banana, salad, basbaas, roasted meat, or yogurt

recipes.review_summary: Canjeero was ingredient-close but the total time ignored fermentation and the optional butter conflicted with vegan tagging. This pass removes butter from the core recipe and makes fermentation honest.
recommended_changes: Ferment the batter for several hours or overnight.
Use flour with sorghum, cornmeal, or starter depending on household style.
Cook one side only so holes form on top.
recipes.official_source_status: Somali sources consistently describe canjeero or lahoh as a fermented, spongy pancake cooked on one side and eaten with sweet or savory toppings.

recipe.description: Somali canjeero, a lightly fermented one-sided pancake with a soft, spongy surface and gentle tang.
recipe.story: The bubbles are the sign. Give the batter time to ferment, then cook it thin and only on one side.
recipe.best_served_with: Sugar, honey, sesame oil, stew, suqaar, tea, or banana

replace_ingredients.name: fine cornmeal, sorghum flour, or whole wheat flour

replace_steps.title: Mix batter
replace_steps.instruction: Whisk flours, sugar, yeast or starter, salt, and warm water into a thin smooth batter.

replace_steps.title: Ferment
replace_steps.instruction: Cover loosely and let the batter ferment until bubbly and lightly sour, several hours or overnight.

replace_steps.title: Adjust texture
replace_steps.instruction: Stir gently and add water if needed. The batter should pour more thinly than pancake batter.

replace_steps.title: Cook one side
replace_steps.instruction: Pour a thin layer into a lightly oiled pan. Cook without flipping until holes form and the surface sets.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Fold and serve warm with sweet toppings or with stew and suqaar.

recipes.review_summary: Suqaar was close. This pass adds xawaash as the core spice cue, keeps peppers and tomatoes, and removes halal as a sourcing guarantee.
recommended_changes: Use small cubes of beef, goat, lamb, or chicken.
Season with xawaash, garlic, onion, peppers, tomato, and chile.
Cook quickly so the meat stays tender.
recipes.official_source_status: Somali recipe references consistently describe suqaar as small-cut meat sauteed with onion, garlic, peppers, tomato, and Somali spices, served with canjeero, rice, pasta, or bread.

recipe.description: Somali suqaar, small-cut meat sauteed with onion, garlic, peppers, tomato, chile, and xawaash.
recipe.story: Suqaar is quick food, so cut the meat small and keep the pan hot. The vegetables should soften into a light sauce around the meat.
recipe.best_served_with: Canjeero, rice, pasta, sabaayad, banana, or basbaas

recipes.review_summary: Bobotie was strong. This pass adds vinegar or lemon for balance, keeps bread-in-milk and egg custard, and names yellow rice and sambals as the proper table.
recommended_changes: Use curried mince with soaked bread, chutney, raisins or sultanas, and bay leaves.
Bake under egg and milk custard.
Serve with yellow rice and sambals.
recipes.official_source_status: South African references consistently describe bobotie as curried minced meat mixed with soaked bread and chutney, baked with an egg-milk topping and bay leaves.

recipe.description: South African bobotie, curried mince with soaked bread, chutney, sultanas, bay leaves, and a golden egg custard topping.
recipe.story: Bobotie is all balance: curry warmth, fruit sweetness, a little acidity, and a custard top that sets softly over the mince.
recipe.best_served_with: Yellow rice, sambals, chutney, banana slices, or salad

recipes.review_summary: Bunny chow was accurate but should not carry a halal guarantee. This pass keeps Durban curry in a hollowed loaf and adds beans as a common vegetarian option.
recommended_changes: Use a quarter or half loaf of white bread.
Fill with Durban-style lamb, chicken, bean, or vegetable curry.
Keep the scooped bread lid for dipping.
recipes.official_source_status: South African references consistently describe bunny chow as a Durban curry served inside a hollowed quarter or half loaf of bread.

sources.title: Penguin Random House South Africa - Bunny Chow PDF

recipe.description: Durban bunny chow, a hollowed loaf filled with lamb, chicken, bean, or vegetable curry and served with the bread lid for scooping.
recipe.story: The bread is the bowl and the utensil. The curry should be thick enough to soak the walls without collapsing the loaf.
recipe.best_served_with: Carrot sambal, chopped onion, cucumber salad, achar, or extra gravy

replace_ingredients.name: lamb, chicken, beef, beans, or vegetables
unsliced white bread
replace_ingredients.prep_note: cut into quarters or halves and hollowed

recipes.review_summary: Chakalaka was good. This pass removes halal as irrelevant and adds cabbage as an optional common vegetable while keeping baked beans, carrots, peppers, curry, and tomato.
recommended_changes: Cook onion, garlic, curry powder, carrots, peppers, tomato, and beans.
Add cabbage if desired.
Serve warm or room temperature with pap and braai.
recipes.official_source_status: South African references consistently describe chakalaka as a spicy vegetable relish with onion, tomato, carrot, peppers, beans, curry spice, and sometimes cabbage.

recipe.description: South African chakalaka, a spicy vegetable relish with carrots, peppers, tomato, baked beans, curry powder, onion, garlic, and chile.
recipe.story: Chakalaka should be bright, spicy, and spoonable. Cook the vegetables until tender but not tired, then let the relish settle before serving.
recipe.best_served_with: Pap, braai meat, boerewors, bread, rice, or samp

recipes.review_summary: South Sudanese asida was incorrectly centered on wheat. This pass changes the base to sorghum, millet, or maize flour and removes halal and ghee from the core side-dish recipe.
recommended_changes: Use sorghum, millet, or maize flour.
Stir into boiling water until smooth and thick.
Shape into a dome with a well for stew.
recipes.official_source_status: South Sudan sources describe asida as a cooked staple made from grains such as sorghum, millet, or corn and served with stew.

recipe.description: South Sudanese asida, a smooth dome of sorghum, millet, or maize flour served with stew in the center.
recipe.story: Asida is about texture. Stir hard until it turns smooth and heavy, then shape it while hot so the stew has a place to pool.
recipe.best_served_with: Bamia, mulah, meat stew, okra sauce, or vegetable stew

replace_steps.title: Boil water
replace_steps.instruction: Bring water to a boil with salt and oil if using.

replace_steps.title: Stir flour
replace_steps.instruction: Lower heat and add flour gradually while stirring hard to prevent lumps.

replace_steps.title: Cook thick
replace_steps.instruction: Keep stirring until the asida is smooth, thick, and pulls from the pot.

replace_steps.title: Shape
replace_steps.instruction: Wet a bowl or spoon, shape into a dome, and make a well for stew.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Spoon bamia, mulah, or another stew into the well and around the base.

recipes.review_summary: Bamia was directionally correct but needed a longer lamb simmer and lemon or lime finish. This pass removes halal as a guarantee.
recommended_changes: Cook lamb until nearly tender before adding okra.
Use tomato, tomato paste, onion, garlic, coriander or cumin, and lemon or lime.
Serve with asida, kisra, or rice.
recipes.official_source_status: South Sudan and Sudan references consistently describe bamia as okra cooked with lamb or meat in tomato, onion, and garlic sauce, served with asida or kisra.

recipe.description: South Sudanese bamia, lamb and okra stew with tomato, onion, garlic, coriander, and a bright lemon finish.
recipe.story: Give the meat its time before the okra joins the pot. That keeps the lamb tender and the okra from disappearing.

replace_ingredients.name: okra
replace_ingredients.prep_note: trimmed, left whole if small or sliced if large

replace_steps.title: Brown meat
replace_steps.instruction: Season and brown the meat in oil until the edges color.

replace_steps.title: Build tomato base
replace_steps.instruction: Add onion, garlic, tomatoes, tomato paste, coriander, and cumin if using. Cook until the tomatoes soften.

replace_steps.title: Simmer meat
replace_steps.instruction: Add water or stock and simmer until the meat is nearly tender.

replace_steps.title: Add okra
replace_steps.instruction: Add okra and cook until tender and the stew thickens.

replace_steps.title: Finish
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in lemon or lime juice and adjust salt before serving.

recipes.review_summary: Kisra with Mulah was missing the real fermentation time and incorrectly carried nut-free while peanut butter was optional. This pass makes the sorghum fermentation and stew timing honest.
recommended_changes: Ferment sorghum batter for at least eight hours.
Cook kisra thin and one-sided.
Serve with mulah, commonly okra or meat stew.
recipes.official_source_status: Sudan and South Sudan references consistently describe kisra as fermented sorghum flatbread served with mullah or stew.

recipe.description: South Sudanese kisra with mulah, fermented sorghum flatbread served with a rich okra and meat stew.
recipe.story: Kisra needs fermentation for its tang and texture. Cook it thin, then use it like edible cloth for scooping the mulah.
recipe.best_served_with: Bamia, meat mulah, okra sauce, lentil stew, or vegetable stew

replace_steps.title: Ferment kisra
replace_steps.instruction: Mix sorghum flour and warm water into a thin batter. Cover and ferment until lightly sour and bubbly.

replace_steps.title: Cook mulah base
replace_steps.instruction: Brown meat in oil, then add onions, garlic, tomatoes, and water or stock. Simmer until the meat is tender.

replace_steps.title: Finish mulah
replace_steps.instruction: Add okra and peanut butter if using. Simmer until the stew thickens.

replace_steps.title: Cook kisra
replace_steps.instruction: Salt the fermented batter, thin if needed, and pour a very thin layer on a hot pan. Cook one side until set.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Fold or roll kisra and serve with mulah for scooping.
